description: This figure presents quantitative assessments of alloying effects on microstructure and physical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steels after solution heat treatment at 1050°C for 2 h followed by water quenching. Panel (a) displays the effect of alloying content on the average κ-carbide diameter (d_Car) plotted against element content in mass percent. The graph shows that both carbon (represented as 10×C%, red squares) and aluminum (Al%, white stars) strongly promote κ-carbide coarsening, with diameters increasing sharply from approximately 1 nm to 50 nm for C and from 0.5 nm to 15 nm for Al. In contrast, manganese (Mn%, blue circles) exhibits a weak inverse relationship, where increasing Mn content from 25% to 35% reduces carbide size from approximately 5 nm to 2 nm. Panel (b) illustrates the linear dependence of mass density (ρ) on alloying content, with the empirical relationship ρ = 8.1 - 0.102C% - 0.1Al% - 0.01Mn% (g/cm³). While all three elements reduce density, aluminum has the strongest effect (steepest slope), followed by carbon and manganese. These data collectively establish that C and Al are the critical elements controlling κ-carbide precipitation kinetics and size, whereas density follows predictable linear mixing behavior based on elemental mass percentages.
